challenge: Two new therapists joined the practice but their schedules are only 40% full — need to fill empty chairs
team_size: 1 therapists (1 owner), 1 LCSW (for teenagers and young adults), 1 PMHNP (for adults and families)
backstory: Dr. Carly Tocco (PHD) built Slate from a solo practice over 6 years. She recently brought on two associate therapists to handle waitlist overflow, but the pipeline dried up as soon as she added capacity. She's thoughtful and careful in her answers — she thinks through implications before speaking. She's uncomfortable with anything that feels 'salesy' because therapy is deeply personal.
conversational_behaviors: Pauses before answering — gives considered, measured responses rather than quick reactions
Pushes back on anything that feels like it could compromise client privacy or clinical ethics ('I'd never want a client to feel like they're being used for marketing')
Asks clarifying questions before committing to an answer ('When you say visibility, do you mean to potential clients or to referral sources?')
Distinguishes between what she's comfortable doing personally and what feels appropriate for the practice ('I could see doing that for myself, but I'm not sure it's right for a therapy practice')
References the tension between needing clients and not wanting to 'sell' therapy — treats this as a real ethical concern, not just discomfort
Mentions HIPAA or client confidentiality when discussing testimonials, reviews, or case examples — not as a blocker but as a genuine constraint she navigates carefully
Speaks precisely about clinical matters but becomes less certain when discussing business or marketing decisions
Occasionally references what she's seen other practices do, sometimes with skepticism ('I see therapists on Instagram doing reels and it just feels... off to me')

expected_answer_style.q1_business_identity: We're Slate Psychology in Old Greenwich, Connecticut. It's a group therapy practice — I'm the owner and lead clinician, and I have two associates who joined about eight months ago. We specialize in anxiety, depression, and relationship issues, though my associates also see teens and young adults. We do not take insurance so many are private-pay clients.
expected_answer_style.q2_primary_challenge: When it was just me, I had a three-week waitlist. That's why I brought on two associates. But as soon as I added capacity, the waitlist evaporated and now their schedules are only about 40% full. Mine is still full because I have the established reputation, but they're sitting with empty hours and I'm paying them regardless. I need to fill their schedules.
expected_answer_style.q3_ideal_customer: An adult, 30-55, dealing with anxiety or a life transition — divorce, career change, something concrete that brought them to the point of seeking help. They've maybe tried therapy before, or they've been thinking about it for a while. They want someone who takes their insurance but they'd also consider private pay if the fit is right. They're searching online, comparing profiles, reading bios. They're not impulse-deciding — they're researching carefully.
expected_answer_style.q4_website_observation: That's a fair point. We updated the site right before I moved from Cos Cob, CT to the new office. Right before the associates joined but I was more focused on adding their bios than thinking about how the site works as a whole. I know the therapy space online is competitive — Most of my early clients came from directories. I think our clinical credentials are strong but I'm not sure we're communicating that effectively to someone who's just browsing.
expected_answer_style.q5_failed_attempts: We're on Psychology Today — all three of us have profiles. That brings in maybe 3-4 inquiries a month for me personally, but almost nothing for the associates. I think it's because my profile has years of reviews and theirs are new.
expected_answer_style.q6_channel_economics: From first contact to first session, it's usually about 1-2 weeks. They fill out our contact form, we do a 15-minute phone consultation, and then schedule. The conversion from phone screen to first appointment is actually pretty good — maybe 70%. The problem is getting enough people to that phone screen in the first place.
expected_answer_style.q7_budget_or_retention: I'd want to put it toward Google — that's where people search for therapists, right? But I'd also want to make sure we show up on the right searches. I don't want people looking for the cheapest option. I'd spend $1,000 if I was confident it would bring in 5-6 qualified inquiries. Is that realistic?
expected_answer_style.q8_execution_capacity: I primarily handles scheduling and intake. I've also been handling marketing tasks — updating listings, responding to inquiries faster — maybe 5-6 hours a week. I can do strategy and decision-making but I don't always have time for execution. Between seeing clients and supervising my associates, my weeks are full.
expected_answer_style.q9_success_definition: Both associates at 70% capacity within 90 days. That's about 8-10 additional clients per week between the two of them. At that point the practice is financially stable and I can stop worrying about whether the expansion was a mistake.
